results.possibleIdentity: Addison Yancy "Pete" Gunter III (1936–2024), commonly known as Dr. Pete A. Y. Gunter, was an American philosopher, academic, and author. He was active at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, in the late 1960s (1965–1969) as an Assistant and Associate Professor of Philosophy. In 1969, he moved to Denton, Texas, to become the founding chairman of the Department of Philosophy at the University of North Texas (then North Texas State University), where he remained until his retirement in 2010. Dr. Gunter earned his B.A. from the University of Texas (1958), a B.A. from Cambridge University as a Marshall Scholar (1960), and his Ph.D. from Yale University (1963). His technical and academic fields included aesthetics, the philosophy of science, and contemporary technology, with a particular emphasis on process philosophy and the works of Henri Bergson and Alfred North Whitehead. He edited the influential book "Bergson and the Evolution of Physics" (1969), published by the University of Tennessee Press. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, a collaboration between artists and scientists/engineers, where he was listed as a technical expert in fields such as aesthetics and philosophy. Gunter was also a significant environmental activist, instrumental in the creation of the Big Thicket National Preserve in Texas.
